settings.title: Neighborhood café fit-out
settings.summary: An empty retail shell turned into a working café in nine weeks — millwork, mechanical, and health-department sign-off included.
settings.description:
First-time café owners with a signed lease and rent starting whether the doors opened or not. Commercial fit-outs are a schedule problem before they're a construction problem, so we planned the job backwards from opening day.

Working backwards from opening day

We ran the health-department requirements first — floor drains, hand sinks, surface specs — so nothing got built twice. The site crew handled mechanical rough-in while the millwork was built off-site, which took three weeks off the critical path.

Every long-lead item, from the espresso machine's dedicated circuit to the storefront glazing, was ordered in week one against a delivery calendar we reviewed with the owners every Friday.

Millwork from our own shop

Running the white-oak service counter through our own shop meant the site dimensions and the millwork never drifted apart. The counter arrived finished, was set in a morning, and the plumber and electrician trimmed it out the same week.

The shelving and window bench use the same white oak, so the room reads as one piece of joinery rather than a collection of fixtures.

Inspections and handover

Passed every inspection on the first visit and handed over keys nine days before the lease's free-rent period ran out.

We stayed on standby through the soft opening for the small stuff: a door closer adjusted, a shelf moved, a faucet cartridge swapped before the first Saturday rush.
